question.text: Scenario: Mr. Rahul Mehta, aged 35, earns Rs. 1,20,000 per month (net take-home). He has the following outstanding debts: (1) Home loan: Rs. 35 lakh outstanding at 8.5% p.a., EMI Rs. 32,000, remaining tenure 15 years; (2) Car loan: Rs. 4,50,000 outstanding at 11% p.a., EMI Rs. 10,000, remaining tenure 5 years; (3) Personal loan: Rs. 2,00,000 outstanding at 16% p.a., EMI Rs. 6,500, remaining tenure 3 years; (4) Credit card outstanding: Rs. 80,000 at 42% p.a. (revolving credit), minimum payment Rs. 4,000/month. His total monthly expenses (excluding debt servicing) are Rs. 45,000. He has recently received an annual bonus of Rs. 3,00,000 and wants advice on how to deploy it. His savings bank balance is Rs. 1,50,000 and he has Rs. 2,00,000 in a liquid mutual fund. Question 1 of 5: What is Rahul's current debt-to-income ratio?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Total monthly debt servicing = 32,000 (home) + 10,000 (car) + 6,500 (personal) + 4,000 (credit card) = Rs. 52,500. DTI = 52,500 / 1,20,000 = 43.75% ≈ 43.8%. **The Trap:** 35.0% might exclude the credit card minimum payment. Include ALL debt servicing obligations. **Cross-Exam Context:** DTI ratio above 35-40% is considered excessive as per X-A (Ch 3 and Ch 4). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Rahul's DTI at 43.8% is in the danger zone — debt reduction should be the immediate priority before any new investments.
